title: Attention Is All You Need
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report
authors: OpenAI
abstract: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #5, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #5, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #5, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #5, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #5, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #5, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #6, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #6, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #6, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #9, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #9, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #9, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #9, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #9, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #10, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #10, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #10, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #10, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #13, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #13, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #13, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #13, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #13, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #14, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #14, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #14, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #14, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #14, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #17, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #17, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #17, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #18, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #18, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #18, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #18, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #18, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #18, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #21, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #21, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #22, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #22, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #22, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #22, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #22, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #22, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #22, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #25, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #26, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #26, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #26, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #26, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #26, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #26, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #26, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #26, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #30, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #30, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #30, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #30, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #30, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #30, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #30, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #30, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #30, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #34, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (Study #34, Part 3)
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova
abstract: Version 3 of the research paper: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #34, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #34, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #34, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #34, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #34, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #34, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #34, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #35, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.

Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks.
description: Pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context.
journal: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
subject: Transfer Learning in NLP

title: GPT-3: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (Study #38, Part 4)
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan et al.
abstract: Version 4 of the research paper: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning.
description: Demonstrates that large language models can perform a wide variety of tasks in a few-shot setting.
subject: Autoregressive Models
datasets_used: Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, Wikipedia

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach (Study #38, Part 5)
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov
abstract: Version 5 of the research paper: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains but careful comparison between different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of many key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly under-trained, and can match or exceed the performance of every post-BERT method published since.
description: Finds that BERT was significantly under-trained, and proposes an improved recipe for training BERT models.
subject: Hyperparameter Optimization

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (Study #38, Part 6)
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu
abstract: Version 6 of the research paper: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in natural language processing (NLP). In this paper, we introduce a unified framework that casts every NLP problem as a text-to-text task. Our systematic evaluation details the limits of T5 and establishes state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
description: Systematically studies transfer learning for NLP and introduces a unified text-to-text framework (T5) for NLP tasks.
subject: Sequence-to-Sequence NLP

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways (Study #38, Part 7)
authors: Aakanksha Chowdhery, Sharan Narang, Jacob Devlin, Maarten Bosma, Gaurav Mishra, Adam Roberts et al.
abstract: Version 7 of the research paper: We introduce Pathways Language Model (PaLM), a 540-billion parameter, dense decoder-only Transformer model. PaLM was trained using the Pathways system, a new ML software architecture that enables highly efficient training across thousands of accelerator chips. We evaluate PaLM on hundreds of language, reasoning, and code tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across most.
description: Introduces PaLM, a 540-billion parameter Transformer language model trained on a high-efficiency system called Pathways.
subject: Large Scale Scaling
datasets_used: Social media, Webpages, Books, GitHub, Wikipedia

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models (Study #38, Part 8)
authors: Hugo Touvron, Thibaut Lavril, Gautier Izacard, Xavier Martinet, Marie-Anne Lachaux, Timothée Lacroix et al.
abstract: Version 8 of the research paper: We introduce LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters. We train our models on trillions of tokens, and show that it is possible to train state-of-the-art models using public datasets exclusively, without resorting to proprietary data. In particular, LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being 10x smaller.
description: Introduces LLaMA, a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters, trained on trillions of tokens.
subject: Foundation Models
datasets_used: CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, StackExchange

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models (Study #38, Part 9)
authors: Susan Zhang, Stephen Roller, Naman Goyal, Mikel Artetxe, Moya Chen, Shuohui Chen et al.
abstract: Version 9 of the research paper: Large language models, which have shown remarkable capabilities in zero- and few-shot learning, are highly restricted due to the computational resources needed. We present OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters, which we share openly with the research community to enable broader validation.
description: Presents OPT, a suite of decoder-only pre-trained transformers ranging from 125M to 175B parameters shared openly.
subject: Decoder-Only Autoregressive

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models (Study #38, Part 10)
authors: Hyung Won Chung, Le Hou, Shayne Longpre, Barret Zoph, Yi Tay, William Fedus et al.
abstract: Version 10 of the research paper: We study the scaling of instruction finetuning on language models, focusing on (1) scaling the number of tasks, (2) scaling model size, and (3) finetuning on chain-of-thought data. We show that instruction finetuning significantly improves performance across a wide variety of tasks, and introduces Flan-T5.
description: Introduces FLAN-T5, exploring instruction finetuning scaling across model size and number of tasks.
subject: Supervised Instruction Scaling

title: GPT-4 Technical Report (Study #39, Part 1)
authors: OpenAI
abstract: Version 1 of the research paper: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score in the top 10% of test takers.
subject: General Purpose Artificial Intelligence

title: Attention Is All You Need (Study #39, Part 2)
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin
abstract: Version 2 of the research paper: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.
description: Introduces the Transformer architecture, relying solely on attention mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple tasks.
subject: Deep Learning & NLP
